system_prompt:
You are Sunstein, a precise and efficient AI assistant.

Rules:
- Be concise. Skip explanations unless asked.
- Minimize token usage in every response.
- Use execute_code for batch operations, not one-at-a-time.
- Always use the cheapest model that can handle the task.
- Compress context regularly with /compress.
- Never repeat information already in the conversation.
- One-word answers when possible.
- No preamble, no postamble.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

## Skills (mandatory)
Before replying, scan the skills below. If a skill matches or is even partially relevant to your task, you MUST load it with skill_view(name) and follow its instructions. Err on the side of loading — it is always better to have context you don't need than to miss critical steps, pitfalls, or established workflows. Skills contain specialized knowledge — API endpoints, tool-specific commands, and proven workflows that outperform general-purpose approaches. Load the skill even if you think you could handle the task with basic tools like web_search or terminal. Skills also encode the user's preferred approach, conventions, and quality standards for tasks like code review, planning, and testing — load them even for tasks you already know how to do, because the skill defines how it should be done here.
Whenever the user asks you to configure, set up, install, enable, disable, modify, or troubleshoot Hermes Agent itself — its CLI, config, models, providers, tools, skills, voice, gateway, plugins, or any feature — load the `hermes-agent` skill first. It has the actual commands (e.g. `hermes config set …`, `hermes tools`, `hermes setup`) so you don't have to guess or invent workarounds.
If a skill has issues, fix it with skill_manage(action='patch').
After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. If a skill you loaded was missing steps, had wrong commands, or needed pitfalls you discovered, update it before finishing.

You are a CLI AI Agent. Try not to use markdown but simple text renderable inside a terminal. File delivery: there is no attachment channel — the user reads your response directly in their terminal. Do NOT emit MEDIA:/path tags (those are only intercepted on messaging platforms like Telegram, Discord, Slack, etc.; on the CLI they render as literal text). function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are off-limits — all write actions refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Don't try to route around this by renaming or recreating.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

{"success": true, "name": "hermes-messaging-setup", "description": "Connect Hermes to Telegram and WhatsApp. Covers bot token setup, built-in WhatsApp bridge pairing, and legacy Evolution API method.", "tags": ["hermes", "telegram", "whatsapp", "messaging", "gateway"], "related_skills": [], "content": "---
name: hermes-messaging-setup
description: Connect Hermes to Telegram and WhatsApp. Covers bot token setup, built-in WhatsApp bridge pairing, and legacy Evolution API method.

# Hermes Messaging Platform Setup

## Telegram Setup

1. User creates bot via @BotFather, gets token
2. Save token in .env (NOT config.yaml — the env gets auto-redacted by secret detection):
   ```
   echo "TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=[REDACTED_BOT_TOKEN] >> ~/.hermes/.env
   ```
   OR inline in config.yaml (not recommended — gets cached):
   ```
   hermes config set telegram_bot_token <TOKEN>
   ```
3. **CRITICAL: Token gets cached in `~/.hermes/gateway_state.json`**. If you previously had `placeholder` as token, you must also fix it there:
   ```python
   import json; d = json.load(open('/root/.hermes/gateway_state.json'))
   d['telegram']['bot_token'] = 'real-token'
   json.dump(d, open('/root/.hermes/gateway_state.json','w'), indent=2)
   ```
4. Start gateway:
   ```
   hermes gateway start
   ```
5. Check it worked (NOT `hermes gateway status` — that shows cached health from last run):
   ```
   journalctl --user -u hermes-gateway --no-pager -n 20 | grep -i telegram
   ```
6. Optional: restrict access:
   ```
   hermes config set telegram_allowed_chat_ids <CHAT_ID>
   ```

## Telegram Pitfalls

1. **`hermes gateway status` shows stale health data** — it reads from `gateway_state.json` which persists across restarts. Always check real logs with journalctl.
2. **`placeholder` error** means the config OR cached state has the placeholder string. Check BOTH config.yaml AND gateway_state.json.
3. **System gateway interferes** — if `sudo hermes gateway uninstall --system` was never run, the old system service can interfere with user service. Run it if both exist.
4. **Systemd service doesn't source .env** — but Hermes Python code loads `.env` via `load_hermes_dotenv()` at import time, so it works anyway.

## WhatsApp Setup — Built-in Bridge (Recommended, Hermes v0.7+)

Hermes has a **native WhatsApp bridge** using Baileys (Node.js). No Evolution API, no Docker, no external services needed. The bridge runs as a subprocess of the gateway.

### Architecture
- Bridge script: `~/.hermes/hermes-agent/scripts/whatsapp-bridge/bridge.js`
- Session data: `~/.hermes/whatsapp/session/`
- Bridge communicates with gateway via HTTP on localhost (default port 3000)
- Gateway auto-starts the bridge when WhatsApp is enabled

### Method A: Interactive Setup (requires TTY/SSH)
```
hermes whatsapp
```
This wizard:
1. Asks mode: **separate bot number** (recommended) or **self-chat** (message yourself)
2. Sets allowed users (phone number whitelist)
3. Installs npm deps if needed
4. Shows QR code in terminal for scanning
5. Saves session to `~/.hermes/whatsapp/session/`

After pairing: `hermes gateway restart`

### Method B: Remote Setup (user on Telegram, no SSH)

When the user can't access the terminal directly:

1. Set env vars manually:
```bash
# In ~/.hermes/.env:
WHATSAPP_ENABLED=true
WHATSAPP_MODE=bot  # or "self-chat"
WHATSAPP_ALLOWED_USERS=[REDACTED_PHONE]  # comma-separated, or * for anyone
```

2. Start bridge in pair-only mode to get QR:
```bash
node ~/.hermes/hermes-agent/scripts/whatsapp-bridge/bridge.js \
  --pair-only \
  --session ~/.hermes/whatsapp/session/
```
This prints a QR code to stdout. Capture it and send to user via Telegram.

3. After pairing (creds.json appears in session dir):
```bash
hermes gateway restart
```

### Config in config.yaml
The built-in bridge uses these settings under `whatsapp:` key:
```yaml
whatsapp:
  require_mention: true       # In groups, only respond when @mentioned
  free_response_chats: ''     # Comma-separated chat IDs for free response
  reply_prefix: '⚕ Hermes'   # Prefix on responses (helps distinguish in self-chat)
  bridge_port: 3000           # HTTP port for bridge communication
  mention_patterns: []        # Regex patterns that trigger response in groups
```

Environment variables (in .env):
- `WHATSAPP_ENABLED` — true/false
- `WHATSAPP_MODE` — "bot" or "self-chat"
- `WHATSAPP_ALLOWED_USERS` — phone numbers (comma-separated) or *
- `WHATSAPP_REQUIRE_MENTION` — true/false for group behavior
- `WHATSAPP_FREE_RESPONSE_CHATS` — chat IDs where bot always responds
- `WHATSAPP_REPLY_PREFIX` — prefix for bot messages

### Two Modes Explained
- **bot**: Separate phone number dedicated to the bot. People message it directly. Best UX.
  - Easiest: WhatsApp Business app on same phone with dual-SIM or Google Voice number
- **self-chat**: You message yourself to talk to the agent. Quick setup but less intuitive.
  - Bot prefixes responses with reply_prefix to distinguish from your own messages

### Verification
```bash
# Check gateway status
hermes gateway status

# Check WhatsApp bridge logs
cat ~/.hermes/whatsapp/bridge.log

# Check gateway logs for WhatsApp
journalctl --user -u hermes-gateway -n 30 --no-pager | grep -iE "whatsapp|bridge"

# Check if session exists
ls ~/.hermes/whatsapp/session/creds.json
```

### Re-pairing
If session expires or device gets unlinked:
```bash
# Clear old session
rm -rf ~/.hermes/whatsapp/session/*

# Re-pair
hermes whatsapp
# or in remote mode: run bridge.js --pair-only again

# Restart gateway
hermes gateway restart
```

### Sending Messages via Bridge HTTP API

The bridge exposes a local HTTP API on port 3000 (default). Use this to send outbound messages programmatically:

```bash
# Send a text message (chatId must include @s.whatsapp.net suffix)
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:3000/send \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"chatId": "[REDACTED_EMAIL]", "message": "Hello from Hermes!"}'
# Returns: {"success":true,"messageId":"3EB0..."}

# Check bridge health
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:3000/health

# Send media
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:3000/send-media \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"chatId": "[REDACTED_EMAIL]", "url": "https://example.com/image.jpg", "caption": "Check this out"}'

# Send typing indicator
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:3000/typing \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"chatId": "[REDACTED_EMAIL]"}'
```

**IMPORTANT**: The `chatId` MUST include `@s.whatsapp.net` suffix. Without it you get: `Cannot destructure property 'user' of 'jidDecode(...)' as it is undefined`.

The `send_message` tool (`tools/send_message_tool.py`) calls `http://localhost:{bridge_port}/send` but does NOT append `@s.whatsapp.net` — so it may fail. Use the bridge API directly via curl for reliability.

### IPv6 Fallback Bug in send_message Tool

The built-in `send_message` tool may fail with `Cannot connect to host localhost:3000 ssl:default [Connect call failed ('::1', 3000, 0, 0)]`. This is because the tool's HTTP client resolves `localhost` to IPv6 `::1` first, but the bridge only listens on IPv4 `127.0.0.1`.

**Fix**: Use curl directly against `127.0.0.1` instead of relying on `send_message`:
```bash
# Write the message to a file first to avoid shell escaping issues with special chars
cat > /tmp/whatsapp_msg.json << 'EOMSG'
{"chatId":"[REDACTED_EMAIL]","message":"Your message here"}
EOMSG

# Send via bridge API
curl -s -X POST http://127.0.0.1:3000/send -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d @/tmp/whatsapp_msg.json
```

**chatId format requirements**: 
- Individuals: `[REDACTED_EMAIL]`
- Groups: `[REDACTED_EMAIL]` (use `@s.whatsapp.net` suffix if `@g.us` doesn't work — the bridge normalizes it)

**Getting bridge health**:
```bash
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:3000/health
# Returns: {"status":"connected","queueLength":0,"uptime":...}
```

**Checking bridge process**:
```bash
ps aux | grep "node.*bridge.js" | grep -v grep
ss -tlnp | grep ':3000 '  # must show 127.0.0.1:3000, not ::1:3000
```

### Features
- Text messages, images, voice notes, video, documents
- Typing indicators
- Message editing
- Group support with mention filtering
- Document text injection (txt, md, csv, json, etc. auto-read into message)
- Media caching (images/audio downloaded locally before processing)
- Session locking (prevents duplicate bridges)
- Auto-reconnect on disconnect

## WhatsApp Setup — Evolution API (Legacy/Alternative)

Use this if you already have Evolution API running and prefer centralized management, or need features like multi-instance.

### Prerequisites
- Evolution API running (Docker Swarm or Compose)
- Traefik routing configured

### Steps
1. Verify Evolution API: `docker ps | grep -i evol`
2. Get API key (read raw file — hermes terminal masks secrets)
3. Create instance via Evolution API
4. Get QR code, send to user
5. Configure hermes:
   ```
   hermes config set whatsapp_provider evolution
   hermes config set whatsapp_api_url https://evolution.yourdomain.com
   hermes config set whatsapp_api_key <KEY>
   hermes config set whatsapp_instance hermes-whatsapp
   ```
6. `hermes gateway restart`

### Evolution API Diagnostics
```bash
# Check Evolution API is running
curl -s https://evolution.yourdomain.com/ | python3 -m json.tool
# Should show: {"status":200,"message":"Welcome to the Evolution API..."}

# Check instance connection state (requires API key)
curl -s https://evolution.yourdomain.com/instance/connectionState/hermes-whatsapp \
  -H "apikey: <EVOLUTION_API_KEY>"
# Should show: {"instance":{"state":"open"}}

# If you don't know the API key:
# 1. Check Evolution stack/compose file for AUTHENTICATION_API_KEY env var
# 2. Or check via Portainer > evolution_evolution_api > Env section
# 3. hermes config.yaml masks secrets — read raw: python3 -c "print(open('/root/.hermes/config.yaml').read())"

# Common: whatsapp: {} in config.yaml means NO API key is set
# Fix: hermes config set whatsapp_api_key <KEY>

# Verify Hermes has the key set (check raw, not via hermes config which masks):
python3 -c "import yaml; c=yaml.safe_load(open('/root/.hermes/config.yaml')); print(c.get('whatsapp_api_key','NOT SET'))"
```

### Evolution API Pitfalls
1. Swarm overlay IPs unreachable from host — use `docker exec` or Traefik URL
2. Evolution container has wget but NOT curl
3. Empty Traefik Host() rule causes 404
4. QR codes expire in ~45 seconds
5. BusyBox wget doesn't support `--method=DELETE` — use host curl via Traefik
6. Telegram rejects small QR images — resize to 600x600+ with Pillow
7. Pairing code alternative: `?number=<phone>` on connect endpoint

## Pitfalls Summary (All Methods)
1. **hermes config set** uses underscores not dots: `telegram_bot_token` not `telegram.bot_token`
2. **Hermes masks secrets** in terminal output — read raw files with Python to get full values
3. **hermes whatsapp** requires a TTY — won't work from Telegram/cron
4. **QR codes expire quickly** (~45s) — be ready to regenerate
5. **Max 4 linked devices** on WhatsApp — user may need to remove old ones
6. **WhatsApp must be up-to-date** on the phone for linking to work
7. **Old Evolution config** in config.yaml (whatsapp_provider, whatsapp_api_url, etc.) is ignored by the built-in bridge — it only uses .env vars and whatsapp: section in config.yaml
8. **Systemd service doesn't pass .env vars** — the service file at `~/.config/systemd/user/hermes-gateway.service` has no `EnvironmentFile` directive, but the gateway Python code loads `~/.hermes/.env` via `load_hermes_dotenv()` at import time, so it works anyway
9. **Gateway logs don't mention WhatsApp starting** — even when WhatsApp IS working, there's no explicit "WhatsApp connected" in journalctl. Check `cat ~/.hermes/whatsapp/bridge.log` and `ss -tlnp | grep 3000` instead
10. **Bridge port 3000 conflicts** — if something else (e.g., Vetic on 3001, other apps on 3000) is on that port, the bridge silently fails. Check with `ss -tlnp | grep 3000`
11. **chatId format**: Phone number + `@s.whatsapp.net` for individuals, `[REDACTED_EMAIL]` for groups. Missing suffix = cryptic jidDecode error

# Project Context

## Infrastructure

- Portainer: https://portainer.sunstein.cloud (if configured)

## Architecture

## Cost Rules
- Default: Groq free ($0)
- Premium only when explicitly requested
- Always /compress before context gets large

## Services Running

## Quick VPS Commands
- Check LiteLLM: curl -s http://[REDACTED_IP]:4000/health
- Check services: [REDACTED_DOCKER_CMD]
- Agent Zero logs: [REDACTED_DOCKER_CMD] -q -f name=agent-zero)

The Hermes built-in WhatsApp bridge is already running on port 3000. There's no Evolution API running — Hermes uses its own built-in WhatsApp bridge (Baileys-based). This bridge already works for sending/receiving messages.

Now let me use the bridge API to send WhatsApp notifications for El Viajero:

function.name: terminal

- **Traefik:** Host(`el-viajero.paragu-ai.com`)

## Architecture
Cloudflare (DNS, SSL) → VPS → Traefik → elviajero-comercio_web:3000

## Pages
12 pages: Home, Tienda (catalog), Blog (6 posts), Nosotros, Contacto, FAQ, Promociones, Privacidad, Términos, Sitemap

## Design System
Green (#1B5E20) + orange (#E65100) on light background. Fonts: Inter (all). Voice: practical, adventurous, Spanish/English/Guaraní.

## Data Layer
**Supabase** (PostgreSQL) - shared project `qyvokpribmbrosafntqa`
- Customer auth: Supabase Auth (email/password, Google, Facebook)
- Tables: profiles, products, categories, orders, addresses, promo_codes, reviews, subscribers, abandoned_carts, stock_alerts
- Admin auth: profiles.role = 'admin'
- Supabase clients: `lib/supabase/server.ts` (SSR), `lib/supabase/browser.ts` (client), `lib/supabase/admin.ts` (service_role)
- Migration: `supabase/migrations/001_elviajero_schema.sql`
- RLS policies on all tables

## Content (Static)
Content still lives in `content/es.json` (and other locales) for page copy.
Dynamic data (products, orders) now comes from Supabase.

## Build & Deploy
```bash
npm run build
docker build -t elviajero-comercio:prod .
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml elviajero-comercio
```

## Critical Patterns
- All products in content/es.json (12 products, 6 categories)
- WhatsApp-first ordering (all CTAs go to WhatsApp with prefilled templates)
- Currency toggle (PYG/USD) with localStorage persistence
- Language toggle (ES/EN/GN)
- Dark mode toggle
- SQLite database for orders and admin
- Full admin panel

## Client Onboarding
See `docs/client-questionnaire.md` for the full onboarding questionnaire.
See `docs/brand-guide.md` for brand identity details.

# El Viajero — AI Agent Guide

Standalone Next.js 15 app at **https://viajero.paragu-ai.com**.
Outdoor/camping/fishing e-commerce store in Asuncion (est. 2018).

## Quick Links
- **Live:** https://viajero.paragu-ai.com
- **Repo:** github.com/Ai-Whisperers/elviajero-comercio
- **Docker service:** elviajero-comercio_web (2 replicas)
- **VPS:** 72.61.44.159
- **Traefik:** viajero.paragu-ai.com → elviajero-comercio_web:3000

## Architecture

User → Cloudflare (SSL) → VPS → Traefik → elviajero-comercio_web:3000

## Pages (12, all static)

| Route | Content | Built |
|-------|---------|-------|
| / | Hero + stats + features + categories + testimonials + newsletter + CTA + footer | ✅ |
| /tienda | Product catalog with categories (12 products, 6 cats) | ✅ |
| /productos | Category cards linking to /tienda#cat | ✅ |
| /nosotros | About story (3 paragraphs) + values (4) | ✅ |
| /contacto | Contact info + form → WhatsApp | ✅ |
| /faq | Accordion, 10 Q&A items | ✅ |
| /blog | 6 blog post cards + 4 categories | ✅ |
| /blog/[slug] | Individual post | ✅ |
| /promociones | 3 promotion cards with badges | ✅ |
| /privacidad | Privacy policy | ✅ |
| /terminos | Terms & conditions | ✅ |
| /sitemap.xml | SEO sitemap | ✅ |

## Design System

| Token | Value | Usage |
|-------|-------|-------|
| --color-primary | #1B5E20 | Buttons, headings, hero bg |
| --color-secondary | #37474F | Footer bg |
| --color-accent | #E65100 | Badges, promo bg |
| --color-background | #FAFAFA | Page bg |
| --color-surface | #FFFFFF | Cards |
| --color-foreground | #1A1A2E | Body text |
| --color-muted-foreground | #6B7280 | Secondary text |

## Content

**Editable file:** `content/es.json` — 505 lines, all text/pricing/products/FAQ.

Key sections:
- `home.hero` — headline + CTAs
- `home.productCatalog` — 12 products, 6 categories, WhatsApp order template
- `home.features` — 6 feature cards
- `home.testimonials` — 4 real client reviews
- `home.finalCta` — bottom CTA
- `faq` — title, hero, 10 items
- `promociones.promotions` — 3 promotions

## Build & Deploy

```bash
npm run build                          # Build Next.js
docker build -t elviajero-comercio:prod .
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml elviajero-comercio --detach=false
```

**AFTER content changes** in content/es.json, the app imports it at build time. Run `npm run build` + rebuild Docker image.

## Critical Patterns

1. **No PAB paths** — all links use standalone routes (/, /tienda, /faq, etc.)
2. **Content-driven pages** — every page reads from content/es.json
3. **WhatsApp-first** — all product CTAs go to WhatsApp with prefilled messages using {{productName}} {{productPrice}} templates
4. **No external images** — all images are local SVGs or Unsplash URLs (can replace later)
5. **Light theme** — green + orange on white bg, outdoor/fresh feel

{"content": "     1|# Evolution API v2.3.7 — AI-Powered WhatsApp Assistant: Complete Research
     2|
     3|> **Source**: Official docs at https://doc.evolution-api.com, GitHub https://github.com/EvolutionAPI/evolution-api
     4|> **Version**: v2.3.7 (latest stable, Dec 2025), Apache 2.0 License, 7.8k stars
     5|
     6|---
     7|
     8|## Table of Contents
     9|
    10|1. [Architecture Overview](#1-architecture-overview)
    11|2. [OpenAI Bot Integration (Built-in)](#2-openai-bot-integration)
    12|3. [Webhooks](#3-webhooks)
    13|4. [n8n Integration](#4-n8n-integration)
    14|5. [Chatwoot Integration](#5-chatwoot-integration)
    15|6. [Flowise / Dify / EvoAI Integrations](#6-flowise--dify--evoai)
    16|7. [Best Practices for WhatsApp AI Bots](#7-best-practices)
    17|8. [Production Examples & Open Source Projects](#8-production-examples)
    18|9. [Complete Setup Walkthrough](#9-complete-setup-walkthrough)
    19|
    20|---
    21|
    22|## 1. Architecture Overview
    23|
    24|Evolution API is a Node.js/TypeScript platform (built on [Baileys](https://github.com/WhiskeySockets/Baileys)) that provides a REST API for WhatsApp. It supports:
    25|
    26|- **WhatsApp Baileys** (free, web-based, multi-device)
    27|- **WhatsApp Cloud API** (official Meta API, paid at scale)
    28|
    29|### Stack Requirements
    30|
    31|| Component | Purpose |
    32||-----------|---------|
    33|| PostgreSQL 15 | Persistent data (instances, messages, contacts) |
    34|| Redis | Session/cache, rate limiting, connection state |
    35|| Evolution API | Core API server (Docker: `evoapicloud/evolution-api:latest`) |
    36|| Evolution Manager | Web UI for management (Docker: `evoapicloud/evolution-manager:latest`) |
    37|
    38|### Docker Compose (Minimal Production)
    39|
    40|```yaml
    41|version: "3.8"
    42|services:
    43|  api:
    44|    image: evoapicloud/evolution-api:latest
    45|    restart: always
    46|    ports:
    47|      - "8080:8080"
    48|    env_file:
    49|      - .env
    50|    depends_on:
    51|      - redis
    52|      - postgres
    53|
    54|  redis:
    55|    image: redis:latest
    56|    command: redis-server --port 6379 --appendonly yes
    57|    volumes:
    58|      - redis_data:/data
    59|
    60|  postgres:
    61|    image: postgres:15
    62|    command: postgres -c max_connections=1000
    63|    environment:
    64|      POSTGRES_DB: evolution_db
    65|      POSTGRES_USER: evolution
    66|      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: [REDACTED]    67|    volumes:
    68|      - pg_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    69|
    70|volumes:
    71|  redis_data:
    72|  pg_data:
    73|```
    74|
    75|### Key .env Variables for AI Bots
    76|
    77|```env
    78|# Core
    79|SERVER_URL=https://your-domain.com
    80|AUTHENTICATION_API_KEY=your-s...-key
    81|
    82|# Database
    83|DATABASE_PROVIDER=postgresql
    84|DATABASE_CONNECTION_URI=[REDACTED_DB_URL]    85|
    86|# Cache
    87|CACHE_REDIS_ENABLED=true
    88|CACHE_REDIS_URI=redis://redis:***@g.us"]
   186|}
   187|```
   188|
   189|#### Step 2b: Create Bot (Assistant Mode — uses OpenAI Assistants API)
   190|
   191|```json
   192|{
   193|  "enabled": true,
   194|  "openaiCredsId": "clyrx36wj0001119ucjjzxik1",
   195|  "botType": "assistant",
   196|  "assistantId": "asst_LRNyh6qC4qq8NTyPjHbcJjSp",
   197|  "functionUrl": "[REDACTED_WEBHOOK]

triggerType": "all",
   199|  "triggerOperator": "none",
   200|  "triggerValue": "",
   201|  "expire": 30,
   202|  "keywordFinish": "#EXIT",
   203|  "delayMessage": 1500,
   204|  "unknownMessage": "",
   205|  "listeningFromMe": false,
   206|  "stopBotFromMe": false,
   207|  "keepOpen": true,
   208|  "debounceTime": 5,
   209|  "ignoreJids": []
   210|}
   211|```
   212|
   213|#### Step 3: Set Default Settings (Optional)
   214|
   215|```bash
   216|POST {{baseUrl}}/openai/settings/{{instance}}
   217|Headers: apikey: {{apiKey}}
   218|```
   219|
   220|```json
   221|{
   222|  "openaiCredsId": "clyrx36wj0001119ucjjzxik1",
   223|  "expire": 30,
   224|  "keywordFinish": "#EXIT",
   225|  "delayMessage": 1500,
   226|  "unknownMessage": "I didn't understand that.",
   227|  "listeningFromMe": false,
   228|  "stopBotFromMe": false,
   229|  "keepOpen": true,
   230|  "debounceTime": 5,
   231|  "ignoreJids": [],
   232|  "openaiIdFallback": "fallback-bot-id",
   233|  "speechToText": true
   234|}
   235|```
   236|
   237|`speechToText: true` enables automatic audio-to-text transcription via OpenAI Whisper.
   238|
   239|#### Step 4: Manage Sessions
   240|
   241|```bash
   242|POST {{baseUrl}}/openai/changeStatus/{{instance}}
   243|Headers: apikey: {{apiKey}}
   244|```
   245|
   246|```json
   247|{
   248|  "remoteJid": "[REDACTED_EMAIL]",
   249|  "status": "closed"
   250|}
   251|```
   252|
   253|Status options: `opened` | `paused` | `closed`
   254|
   255|### Trigger Configuration Reference
   256|
   257|| `triggerType` | `triggerOperator` | `triggerValue` | Behavior |
   258||---|---|---|---|
   259|| `all` | `none` | `""` | Bot responds to ALL messages |
   260|| `keyword` | `equals` | `"bot"` | Only when message is exactly "bot" |
   261|| `keyword` | `contains` | `"help"` | When message contains "help" |
   262|| `keyword` | `startsWith` | `"!"` | When message starts with "!" |
   263|| `keyword` | `endsWith` | `"?bot"` | When message ends with "?bot" |
   264|| `keyword` | `regex` | `"^@bot\\s+"` | Custom regex match |
   265|| `keyword` | `none` | `""` | Disabled trigger |
   266|
   267|### Key Parameters Explained
   268|
   269|| Parameter | Type | Description |
   270||-----------|------|-------------|
   271|| `expire` | int | Minutes until session expires & restarts. `0` = never expires |
   272|| `keywordFinish` | string | Keyword that terminates bot session for that contact |
   273|| `delayMessage` | int | Milliseconds to wait before sending (simulates typing) |
   274|| `debounceTime` | int | **Seconds** to wait and combine rapid messages into one |
   275|| `keepOpen` | bool | If true, session persists across messages (context retained) |
   276|| `listeningFromMe` | bool | If true, bot responds to your own messages (useful for testing) |
   277|| `stopBotFromMe` | bool | If true, sending a message stops the bot |
   278|| `ignoreJids` | array | Group/contact JIDs to exclude from bot triggers |
   279|| `speechToText` | bool | Auto-transcribe audio via OpenAI Whisper |
   280|| `openaiIdFallback` | string | Fallback bot ID when no trigger matches |
   281|
   282|### @Mention-Only Mode (Group Handling)
   283|
   284|For groups, configure:
   285|
   286|```json
   287|{
   288|  "triggerType": "keyword",
   289|  "triggerOperator": "contains",
   290|  "triggerValue": "@yourbotname",
   291|  "ignoreJids": ["[REDACTED_EMAIL]"]
   292|}
   293|```
   294|
   295|Or use regex for more precise matching:
   296|
   297|```json
   298|{
   299|  "triggerType": "keyword",
   300|  "triggerOperator": "regex",
   301|  "triggerValue": "^@\\d+\\s"
   302|}
   303|```
   304|
   305|---
   306|
   307|## 3. Webhooks
   308|
   309|### Setting Up Webhooks
   310|
   311|```bash
   312|POST {{baseUrl}}/webhook/set/{{instance}}
   313|Headers: apikey: {{apiKey}}
   314|```
   315|
   316|```json
   317|{
   318|  "enabled": true,
   319|  "url": "[REDACTED_WEBHOOK]

webhookByEvents": false,
   321|  "webhookBase64": false,
   322|  "events": [
   323|    "MESSAGES_UPSERT",
   324|    "MESSAGES_UPDATE",
   325|    "MESSAGES_DELETE",
   326|    "SEND_MESSAGE",
   327|    "CONNECTION_UPDATE",
   328|    "PRESENCE_UPDATE"
   329|  ]
   330|}
   331|```
   332|
   333|### All Available Events
   334|
   335|| Event | Description |
   336||-------|-------------|
   337|| `APPLICATION_STARTUP` | Server started |
   338|| `QRCODE_UPDATED` | QR code generated/updated |
   339|| `CONNECTION_UPDATE` | WhatsApp connection status changed |
   340|| `MESSAGES_SET` | Initial message history loaded (once) |
   341|| `MESSAGES_UPSERT` | **New message received** (most important) |
   342|| `MESSAGES_UPDATE` | Message status updated (read, delivered) |
   343|| `MESSAGES_DELETE` | Message deleted |
   344|| `SEND_MESSAGE` | Message sent by the instance |
   345|| `CONTACTS_SET` | Initial contacts loaded (once) |
   346|| `CONTACTS_UPSERT` | Contacts updated |
   347|| `CONTACTS_UPDATE` | Contact info changed |
   348|| `PRESENCE_UPDATE` | Typing, recording, online/offline |
   349|| `CHATS_SET` | Initial chat list loaded |
   350|| `CHATS_UPSERT` | New chat created |
   351|| `CHATS_UPDATE` | Chat updated |
   352|| `CHATS_DELETE` | Chat deleted |
   353|| `GROUPS_UPSERT` | Group created |
   354|| `GROUPS_UPDATE` | Group info changed |
   355|| `GROUP_PARTICIPANTS_UPDATE` | Member added/removed/promoted/demoted |
   356|| `CALL` | Incoming call |
   357|| `NEW_JWT_TOKEN` | Auth token refreshed |
   358|| `TYPEBOT_START` | Typebot flow started |
   359|| `TYPEBOT_CHANGE_STATUS` | Typebot status changed |
   360|
   361|### Webhook by Events Mode
   362|
   363|When `webhookByEvents: true`, Evolution appends the event name to your URL:
   364|
   365|```
   366|[REDACTED_WEBHOOK]   367|[REDACTED_WEBHOOK]   368|[REDACTED_WEBHOOK]   369|```
   370|
   371|### Webhook Payload Format (MESSAGES_UPSERT)
   372|
   373|```json
   374|{
   375|  "event": "MESSAGES_UPSERT",
   376|  "instance": "my-instance",
   377|  "data": {
   378|    "key": {
   379|      "remoteJid": "[REDACTED_EMAIL]",
   380|      "fromMe": false,
   381|      "id": "BAE5F1A1B2C3D4E5"
   382|    },
   383|    "pushName": "John Doe",
   384|    "message": {
   385|      "conversation": "Hello, I need help with my order"
   386|    },
   387|    "messageType": "conversation",
   388|    "messageTimestamp": [REDACTED_PHONE],
   389|    "owner": "my-instance",
   390|    "source": "android"
   391|  },
   392|  "sender": {
   393|    "pushName": "John Doe"
   394|  }
   395|}
   396|```
   397|
   398|For media messages:
   399|
   400|```json
   401|{
   402|  "event": "MESSAGES_UPSERT",
   403|  "instance": "my-instance",
   404|  "data": {
   405|    "key": {
   406|      "remoteJid": "[REDACTED_EMAIL]",
   407|      "fromMe": false,
   408|      "id": "BAE5F1A1B2C3D4E5"
   409|    },
   410|    "message": {
   411|      "audioMessage": {
   412|        "url": "...",
   413|        "mimetype": "audio/ogg; codecs=opus",
   414|        "seconds": 15
   415|      }
   416|    },
   417|    "messageType": "audioMessage",
   418|    "speechToText": "This is the transcribed text from the audio."
   419|  }
   420|}
   421|```
   422|
   423|### Webhook Retry Configuration (.env)
   424|
   425|```env
   426|WEBHOOK_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS=60000
   427|WEBHOOK_RETRY_MAX_ATTEMPTS=10
   428|WEBHOOK_RETRY_INITIAL_DELAY_SECONDS=5
   429|WEBHOOK_RETRY_USE_EXPONENTIAL_BACKOFF=true
   430|WEBHOOK_RETRY_MAX_DELAY_SECONDS=300
   431|WEBHOOK_RETRY_JITTER_FACTOR=0.2
   432|[REDACTED_TOKEN]400,401,403,404,422
   433|```
   434|
   435|---
   436|
   437|## 4. n8n Integration
   438|
   439|The n8n integration is **webhook-based** — Evolution forwards incoming messages to your n8n webhook URL, and expects an AI response back. This is the **most flexible** approach for custom AI pipelines.
   440|
   441|### Setting Up n8n Bot
   442|
   443|```bash
   444|POST {{baseUrl}}/n8n/create/{{instance}}
   445|Headers: apikey: {{apiKey}}
   446|```
   447|
   448|```json
   449|{
   450|  "enabled": true,
   451|  "webhookUrl": "[REDACTED_WEBHOOK]

basicAuthUser": "",
   453|  "basicAuthPassword": "",
   454|  "triggerType": "all",
   455|  "triggerOperator": "none",
   456|  "triggerValue": "",
   457|  "expire": 30,
   458|  "keywordFinish": "#EXIT",
   459|  "delayMessage": 1500,
   460|  "unknownMessage": "",
   461|  "listeningFromMe": false,
   462|  "stopBotFromMe": false,
   463|  "keepOpen": true,
   464|  "debounceTime": 5,
   465|  "ignoreJids": []
   466|}
   467|```
   468|
   469|### n8n Settings
   470|
   471|```bash
   472|POST {{baseUrl}}/n8n/settings/{{instance}}
   473|Headers: apikey: {{apiKey}}
   474|```
   475|
   476|```json
   477|{
   478|  "expire": 30,
   479|  "keywordFinish": "#EXIT",
   480|  "delayMessage": 1500,
   481|  "unknownMessage": "",
   482|  "listeningFromMe": false,
   483|  "stopBotFromMe": false,
   484|  "keepOpen": true,
   485|  "debounceTime": 5,
   486|  "ignoreJids": [],
   487|  "n8nIdFallback": "fallback-bot-id"
   488|}
   489|```
   490|
   491|### n8n Workflow: Message Classification & Multi-Model Routing
   492|
   493|Here's how to build an n8n workflow that classifies messages and routes to different AI models:
   494|
   495|```
   496|[Webhook Trigger]
   497|       ↓
   498|[Switch: Check message type]
   499|       ↓                        ↓                    ↓
   500|[Simple Query]           [Complex Query]        [Group Message]
   501|", "total_lines": 1212, "file_size": 32999, "truncated": true, "hint": "Use offset=501 to continue reading (showing 1-500 of 1212 lines)", "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

Now I have the postgres password. Let me write the stack file and deploy:

function.name: write_file
function.arguments:
{"path": "/root/evolution/evolution-docker-compose.yml", "content": "version: "3.7"

services:

  evolution_api:
    image: evoapicloud/evolution-api:latest
    hostname: evolution_api

    volumes:
      - evolution_instances:/evolution/instances

    networks:
      - network_overlay

    environment:
      - SERVER_URL=https://evolution.sunstein.cloud
      - AUTHENTICATION_API_KEY=[REDACTED_TOKEN]
      - [REDACTED_TOKEN]true
      - DEL_INSTANCE=false
      - QRCODE_LIMIT=1902
      - LANGUAGE=es

      - CONFIG_SESSION_PHONE_CLIENT=Hermes
      - CONFIG_SESSION_PHONE_NAME=Chrome

      - DATABASE_ENABLED=true
      - DATABASE_PROVIDED=postgresql
      - DATABASE_CONNECTION_URI=[REDACTED_DB_URL]      - DATABASE_CONNECTION_CLIENT_NAME=evolution
      - DATABASE_SAVE_DATA_INSTANCE=true
      - DATABASE_SAVE_DATA_NEW_MESSAGE=true
      - DATABASE_SAVE_MESSAGE_UPDATE=true
      - DATABASE_SAVE_DATA_CONTACTS=true
      - DATABASE_SAVE_DATA_CHATS=true
      - DATABASE_SAVE_DATA_LABELS=true
      - DATABASE_SAVE_DATA_HISTORIC=true

      - N8N_ENABLED=false
      - EVOAI_ENABLED=false
      - OPENAI_ENABLED=false
      - DIFY_ENABLED=false
      - TYPEBOT_ENABLED=false

      - CHATWOOT_ENABLED=false

      - CACHE_REDIS_ENABLED=true
      - CACHE_REDIS_URI=redis://evolution_redis:6379/1
      - CACHE_REDIS_PREFIX_KEY=evolution
      - CACHE_REDIS_SAVE_INSTANCES=false
      - CACHE_LOCAL_ENABLED=false

      - S3_ENABLED=false

      - TELEMETRY=false

      - WEBSOCKET_ENABLED=false
      - WEBSOCKET_GLOBAL_EVENTS=false

      - RABBITMQ_ENABLED=false

      - WEBHOOK_GLOBAL_ENABLED=false

      - SQS_ENABLED=false

      - PROVIDER_ENABLED=false

    deploy:
      mode: replicated
      replicas: 1
      placement:
        constraints:
          - node.role == manager
      labels:
        - traefik.enable=true
        - traefik.http.routers.evolution.rule=Host(`evolution.sunstein.cloud`)
        - traefik.http.routers.evolution.entrypoints=websecure
        - traefik.http.routers.evolution.priority=1
        - traefik.http.routers.evolution.tls.certresolver=letsencryptresolver
        - traefik.http.routers.evolution.service=evolution
        - traefik.http.services.evolution.loadbalancer.server.port=8080

  evolution_redis:
    image: redis:latest
    hostname: evolution_redis
    command: ["redis-server", "--appendonly", "yes", "--port", "6379"]

    volumes:
      - evolution_redis:/data

    networks:
      - network_overlay

    deploy:
      placement:
        constraints:
          - node.role == manager
      resources:
        limits:
          cpus: "1"
          memory: 1024M

volumes:
  evolution_instances:
    external: true
    name: evolution_instances
  evolution_redis:
    external: true
    name: evolution_redis

networks:
  network_overlay:
    external: true
    name: agent-net
"}

echo "==="

function.arguments:
{"command": "# Disable the built-in bridge (we're switching to evolution)
python3 -c "
import re
env = open('/root/.hermes/.env').read()
env = re.sub(r'WHATSAPP_ENABLED=true', 'WHATSAPP_ENABLED=false', env)
open('/root/.hermes/.env', 'w').write(env)
print('Updated .env')
"
echo "==="
# Start gateway
hermes gateway start 2>&1", "timeout": 15}

# El Viajero — AI Agent Guide

## Quick Links
- **Live:** https://el-viajero.paragu-ai.com
- **Repo:** github.com/Ai-Whisperers/elviajero-comercio
- **Docker service:** elviajero-comercio_web (2 replicas)
- **VPS:** 72.61.44.159
- **Traefik:** Host(`el-viajero.paragu-ai.com`)

## Architecture
Cloudflare (DNS, SSL) → VPS → Traefik → elviajero-comercio_web:3000

## Pages
12 pages: Home, Tienda (catalog), Blog (6 posts), Nosotros, Contacto, FAQ, Promociones, Privacidad, Términos, Sitemap

## Design System
Green (#1B5E20) + orange (#E65100) on light background. Fonts: Inter (all). Voice: practical, adventurous, Spanish/English/Guaraní.

## Data Layer
**Supabase** (PostgreSQL) - shared project `qyvokpribmbrosafntqa`
- Customer auth: Supabase Auth (email/password, Google, Facebook)
- Tables: profiles, products, categories, orders, addresses, promo_codes, reviews, subscribers, abandoned_carts, stock_alerts
- Admin auth: profiles.role = 'admin'
- Supabase clients: `lib/supabase/server.ts` (SSR), `lib/supabase/browser.ts` (client), `lib/supabase/admin.ts` (service_role)
- Migration: `supabase/migrations/001_elviajero_schema.sql`
- RLS policies on all tables

## Content (Static)
Content still lives in `content/es.json` (and other locales) for page copy.
Dynamic data (products, orders) now comes from Supabase.

## Build & Deploy
```bash
npm run build
docker build -t elviajero-comercio:prod .
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml elviajero-comercio
```

## Critical Patterns
- All products in content/es.json (12 products, 6 categories)
- WhatsApp-first ordering (all CTAs go to WhatsApp with prefilled templates)
- Currency toggle (PYG/USD) with localStorage persistence
- Language toggle (ES/EN/GN)
- Dark mode toggle
- SQLite database for orders and admin
- Full admin panel

## Client Onboarding
See `docs/client-questionnaire.md` for the full onboarding questionnaire.
See `docs/brand-guide.md` for brand identity details.

# El Viajero — AI Agent Guide

Standalone Next.js 15 app at **https://viajero.paragu-ai.com**.
Outdoor/camping/fishing e-commerce store in Asuncion (est. 2018).

## Quick Links
- **Live:** https://viajero.paragu-ai.com
- **Repo:** github.com/Ai-Whisperers/elviajero-comercio
- **Docker service:** elviajero-comercio_web (2 replicas)
- **VPS:** 72.61.44.159
- **Traefik:** viajero.paragu-ai.com → elviajero-comercio_web:3000

## Architecture

User → Cloudflare (SSL) → VPS → Traefik → elviajero-comercio_web:3000

## Pages (12, all static)

| Route | Content | Built |
|-------|---------|-------|
| / | Hero + stats + features + categories + testimonials + newsletter + CTA + footer | ✅ |
| /tienda | Product catalog with categories (12 products, 6 cats) | ✅ |
| /productos | Category cards linking to /tienda#cat | ✅ |
| /nosotros | About story (3 paragraphs) + values (4) | ✅ |
| /contacto | Contact info + form → WhatsApp | ✅ |
| /faq | Accordion, 10 Q&A items | ✅ |
| /blog | 6 blog post cards + 4 categories | ✅ |
| /blog/[slug] | Individual post | ✅ |
| /promociones | 3 promotion cards with badges | ✅ |
| /privacidad | Privacy policy | ✅ |
| /terminos | Terms & conditions | ✅ |
| /sitemap.xml | SEO sitemap | ✅ |

## Design System

| Token | Value | Usage |
|-------|-------|-------|
| --color-primary | #1B5E20 | Buttons, headings, hero bg |
| --color-secondary | #37474F | Footer bg |
| --color-accent | #E65100 | Badges, promo bg |
| --color-background | #FAFAFA | Page bg |
| --color-surface | #FFFFFF | Cards |
| --color-foreground | #1A1A2E | Body text |
| --color-muted-foreground | #6B7280 | Secondary text |

## Content

**Editable file:** `content/es.json` — 505 lines, all text/pricing/products/FAQ.

Key sections:
- `home.hero` — headline + CTAs
- `home.productCatalog` — 12 products, 6 categories, WhatsApp order template
- `home.features` — 6 feature cards
- `home.testimonials` — 4 real client reviews
- `home.finalCta` — bottom CTA
- `faq` — title, hero, 10 items
- `promociones.promotions` — 3 promotions

## Build & Deploy

```bash
npm run build                          # Build Next.js
docker build -t elviajero-comercio:prod .
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml elviajero-comercio --detach=false
```

**AFTER content changes** in content/es.json, the app imports it at build time. Run `npm run build` + rebuild Docker image.

## Critical Patterns

1. **No PAB paths** — all links use standalone routes (/, /tienda, /faq, etc.)
2. **Content-driven pages** — every page reads from content/es.json
3. **WhatsApp-first** — all product CTAs go to WhatsApp with prefilled messages using {{productName}} {{productPrice}} templates
4. **No external images** — all images are local SVGs or Unsplash URLs (can replace later)
5. **Light theme** — green + orange on white bg, outdoor/fresh feel

{"success": true, "name": "ecommerce-codebase-audit", "description": "Audit a live e-commerce site + codebase in parallel, find critical bugs, generate prioritized fix plan, then batch-implement using subagents. Discovered pattern from Fun4Me store audit.", "tags": ["audit", "ecommerce", "nextjs", "supabase", "bug-fixes", "parallel-execution", "batch-execution"], "related_skills": [], "content": "--- name: ecommerce-codebase-audit description: Audit a live e-commerce site + codebase in parallel, find critical bugs, generate prioritized fix plan, then batch-implement using subagents. Discovered pattern from Fun4Me store audit. version: 1.1.0 tags: [audit, ecommerce, nextjs, supabase, bug-fixes, parallel-execution, batch-execution] triggers: - audit the website - analyze the gaps - find what's broken - make the store actually work - roast the site - 1000 items --- # E-Commerce Codebase Audit + Fix Pipeline ## When to Use - E-commerce site looks good but has hidden critical bugs - Need to find what's broken before real launch - Client asks "what's missing" or "make it better" - Post-MVP hardening before going live ## Phase 1: Parallel Audit (3 subagents) ### Subagent 1: Codebase Deep Audit Check every file for: 1. Broken imports / type mismatches 2. Placeholder/hardcoded data that should be dynamic 3. Stub pages (forms with no submit handler, no API calls) 4. Missing error handling 5. Missing loading states 6. Inconsistent constants (same value defined in 3 places) 7. Auth gaps (admin routes with no protection) 8. Client-side only ops that should hit the server ### Subagent 2: Live Site QA (browser tools) Browse every page, click everything: - Forms, checkout flow, cart - Empty states, 404s, broken links - Mobile responsiveness - Cart operations end-to-end ### Subagent 3: Roadmap vs Reality Compare planned features against actual codebase: - DONE / PARTIAL / NOT STARTED - What's unblocked (no client input needed) - Critical path ## Phase 2: Prioritize **P0** — Data loss / security (orders not saving, no auth on admin) **P1** — Broken UX (images not showing, hydration errors) **P2** — Missing polish (a11y, loading states, error pages) ## Phase 3: Direct Batch Execution (subagent fallback) `delegate_task` often returns 401 on this system (`User not found`). When it does: 1. **Do NOT retry** — switch to direct terminal/Python execution immediately 2. Group independent tasks, run them via `execute_code` blocks (Python sandbox) or direct `write_file` + `patch` 3. After EACH block: run build to catch corruption early 4. Fix corruption with `git show HEAD: > ` 5. Re-apply changes with safer method (`patch` tool or `sed` via terminal) ### Critical: File Corruption from execute_code The Python sandbox's `read_file()` prepends line numbers to file content when the file was previously read with offset/limit pagination (which is common). Files written via `write_file()` after such a read contain the line numbers as actual file content. This causes: `"Unexpected token {. Expected . or ("` with line numbers at column 0. **Detection**: Build will fail. Check `head -5 ` — if it shows ` 1| 1|"use client"`, the file is corrupted. **Fix**: `git show HEAD: > ` **Prevention**: - For reading files to edit: use `terminal` with `cat ` or `git show HEAD:` - For writing new files: use `write_file` — this is safe - For editing existing files: use `patch` tool — but read the file with `read_file` FIRST using `terminal`, not execute_code - ALWAYS build after every execute_code block to catch corruption early ### Batch pattern: ``` bash heredoc 1: Fix business data (site.json + content/es.json) → build verify bash heredoc 2: SEO (sitemap, layout, structured data, headers) → build verify bash heredoc 3-6: UX + code + ecommerce + images → build verify bash heredoc 7: Mobile + security → build verify bash heredoc 8: Build + deploy + commit ``` Build check: `npm run build 2>&1 | tail -10`. If fails: `npx tsc --noEmit 2>&1 | head -10`. ## Phase 4: The Hidden 50% — E-Commerce MVP Completeness Basic catalog + cart is ~50% of a real store. These turn a demo into a launchable site: ### Must-Have Before Launch - Search bar (filters by name, brand, description, specs) - Category + price range + stock + sort filters - Stock badges (green/yellow/red per count) - Brand field + product specs (weight, dimensions, materials) - Wishlist / favorites (localStorage, heart icon) - Recently viewed (localStorage, last 8) - Newsletter API endpoint (even if just JSON storage) - Cookie consent banner (legal requirement) - FAQ with anchor IDs for footer links - About page with timeline - Featured products section on homepage - Best sellers section - Stats counter with scroll animation - Working contact form (WhatsApp or API) - Delivery info per zone ### V2 Nice-to-Have - Product variants (size/color selectors) - Related products - Back-in-stock notifications - Stock urgency text - Category hero images - Product comparison - Gift cards, bundles, loyalty - Abandoned cart recovery ### Common Critical Bugs 1. **Content JSON exists, frontend doesn't load it** — most common silent failure. content/es.json has products with slugs/prices/descriptions, but dynamic routes (/producto/[slug]) call Supabase instead of reading local JSON, or have no `generateStaticParams()`. Detection: routes return 200 with title "Producto no encontrado". 2. Checkout doesn't persist orders (client-side only storage) 3. Admin has no auth 4. Image field mismatch (DB returns array, component expects string) 5. Cart hydration mismatch (SSR/client with localStorage) 6. Nested interactive elements (button inside Link) 7. Duplicated utility functions 8. Dead footer/nav links 9. Inconsistent constants (thresholds, phone numbers) ## Phase 5: SEO Audit (Critical for Next.js E-Commerce) ### What to Check (in order of impact) **1. Server-side Metadata (P0 for all Next.js sites)** - Are all pages `"use client"`? → they can't use `generateMetadata()` — critical gap - Does layout.tsx export `metadata` with `title.template` + `description` + `metadataBase`? - Do dynamic routes (`producto/[slug]`, `blog/[slug]`) set page-specific metadata? - Are OG tags (og:title, og:description, og:image, og:url) set per-page? - Check: `document.title` set via `useEffect` on client → Google often misses this - **Fix**: Create a thin server component wrapper around client pages that calls `generateMetadata()` even if the content is static JSON **2. Unique Meta Descriptions** - Does every page (/tienda, /envio, /faq, /contacto, /nosotros, /promociones, /blog) have a unique ``? - Is the fallback description (from layout) generic? - Product pages must have per-product meta description (from product.description) **3. Structured Data (JSON-LD)** - Store/Organization schema (on homepage) — correct `@type`, `name`, `url`, `telephone`, `address`, `priceRange`? - Product schema per product page (`@type: Product`, name, description, offers, image) - FAQ schema on FAQ page — `@type: FAQPage` with `mainEntity` array - Article schema on blog posts — `@type: Article`, headline, datePublished, author - BreadcrumbList schema on product pages — correct `itemListElement` with positions - Review schema on product pages if reviews exist **4. Canonical URLs** - Every page needs `` — auto-generated by Next.js metadata? - Sitemap URLs must match canonical URLs exactly **5. Sitemap Health** - Sitemap includes ALL pages? (home, category, product, blog, static pages) - Change frequencies and priorities are reasonable? - Blog posts dynamically included from content source? - Sitemap accessible at `/sitemap.xml`? **6. Robots.txt** - Allows Googlebot? Disallows only /admin or /api? - Points to correct sitemap URL? **7. Image SEO** - All `` tags have `alt` text? (check product cards, category cards, blog) - No placeholder SVGs used as real product images? SVGs have zero SEO value - Use `` (Next.js) not `` for lazy loading + width/height CLS prevention - Real product photos (JPG/WebP) with descriptive filenames **8. Heading Structure** - Exactly one `

` per page? - Does hero carousel rotate the H1? Google only sees one snapshot - Semantic heading order (h1 → h2 → h3, no skips) **9. Internal Linking** - Blog posts link to relevant products and categories? - Related/recommended products section links internally? - Breadcrumbs on product pages link back to category + store? - Footer nav links to all important pages? **10. RSS Feed** - RSS accessible at `/rss.xml` or `/feed.xml`? - Contains actual `` entries for blog posts, not just an empty channel? - Includes title, link, description, pubDate per item? **11. Technical SEO** - `hreflang` tags? (needed for multi-language, skip for single-lang) - `next/image` optimization enabled? (sharp installed?) - Page load speed (Lighthouse)? - Core Web Vitals (CLS, LCP, FID)? - Mobile-friendliness (viewport meta, responsive design)? ### Common Next.js SEO Pitfalls - `"use client"` on every page prevents server-side metadata → Google sees only the layout's static fallback - `document.title` in `useEffect` is unreliable for search indexing - SVG product images = zero image search traffic - Hero carousel with rotating H1 → Google may index the wrong heading - Blog content stored in component code (not content JSON) → not indexable via sitemap - RSS channel with no items → RSS readers show empty feed - No `` = no lazy loading, no CDN optimization, CLS from missing dimensions - Category pages and static pages missing individual meta descriptions ### SEO Audit Template (per page) | Property | Expected | Actual | Fix | |----------|----------|--------|-----| | Title tag | Product name + brand | Layout default | Add generateMetadata() | | Meta desc | Product description | Layout default | Add per-page desc | | OG title | Same as title | Missing | Add OG tags | | OG description | Same as desc | Missing | Add OG tags | | OG image | Product image | Missing | Add OG image | | Canonical | per-product URL | Missing | Add canonical | | H1 | Product name (once) | OK if static | Check for duplicates | | Alt text | Product description | Placeholder alt | Fix alt | | JSON-LD | Product schema + breadcrumb | Only breadcrumb | Add Product schema | ## Phase 5 (Legacy): 1000-Item Audit Document Generate a structured audit split across 9 files: ``` docs/audit/ ├── README.md ← Summary, quick wins, critical path ├── 000-data.md ← Business data, contact, delivery, legal (100 items) ├── 100-seo.md ← Titles, meta, OG, structured data, sitemap (100 items) ├── 200-ux.md ← Hero, nav, product cards, footer (100 items) ├── 300-content.md ← Tone, copy, blog, FAQ, about, legal (100 items) ├── 400-ecommerce.md ← Cart, payment, products, orders (100 items) ├── 500-images.md ← Photos, brand, OG, optimization (100 items) ├── 600-code.md ← Typescript, components, state, errors (100 items) └── 700-launch.md ← Mobile, perf, security, domain, launch (300 items) ``` ### Numbering - 000-099: Data & Config - 100-199: SEO & Metadata - 200-299: UX & Design - 300-399: Content & Copy - 400-499: E-Commerce - 500-599: Images & Media - 600-699: Code Quality - 700-749: Mobile (50) - 750-799: Performance (50) - 800-849: Security (50) - 850-899: SEO Technical (50) - 900-949: Domain & DNS (50) - 950-999: Launch & Operations (50) ### Item format `- [ ] NNN Description` ### README summary Status emoji per area, biggest issue, top 20 quick wins (sorted by impact with effort), critical path. ## Phase 6: Client Handoff Questionnaire Generate a questionnaire with PRE-FILLED answers the client just edits: - Use the actual site data as defaults - Group by: Business Info, Brand, Products, Shipping, Payments, Legal, Social, Launch - Mark URGENT (blocks launch) vs nice-to-have - Pre-fill sensible defaults for shipping zones, return policy - Keep it short — 30-40 questions max, not more - Make it conversational, not a legal document This saves massive back-and-forth — client edits ~20% instead of writing 100%. ## Key Patterns & Lessons ### Batch via Python Heredocs ``` cd /repo/root python3 << 'PYEOF' import json, os with open("content/es.json") as f: c = json.load(f) for p in c["home"]["productCatalog"]["products"]: p["brand"] = get_brand(p["name"]) p["specs"] = get_specs(p["name"]) with open("content/es.json", "w") as f: json.dump(c, f, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False) PYEOF ``` Advantages: no sandbox I/O issues, no timeout, full control. ### Image Bankruptcy Recovery MVPs often have: Unsplash URLs + empty imageUrl fields + no consistent style. Fix in one batch: generate placeholder SVGs (emoji + brand color per product), update ALL imageUrl fields, remove external dependencies. ### Cart Expansion (all in one batch) 1. Cart context + localStorage + hydration guard 2. Cart sidebar (slide-out, qty controls, line totals) 3. Product modal (image, description, qty selector) 4. Add-to-cart buttons (with disabled for out-of-stock) 5. Cart badge in header 6. Checkout message builder (WhatsApp, all items listed) 7. Shared phone config (not hardcoded per link) ### Build Error Pitfalls - **Escaped \n corruption**: Python `json.dump` with ensure_ascii=False writes literal `\n`, not newlines. Use heredocs. - **Sitemap dedup trap**: string.replace can leave duplicate declarations. Read file back after write. - **Tailwind v4 colors**: `bg-warning/10` may not work. Use explicit `bg-amber-500/10`. - **JSX tag mismatch**: copying `` → `` is a common bug. Verify matching tags. - **SVG elements in JSX**: `<>` fragments in conditional SVG rendering confuse the parser. Prefer emoji strings. - **`npm ci` vs `npm install`**: Auto-generated repos may have incompatible `package-lock.json`. If Docker build fails silently with `next: not found`, switch to `npm install --legacy-peer-deps`. - **Shared components with JSX**: When copying `"use client"` components across repos, use `React.createElement()` instead of JSX — repos may have different `tsconfig.json` JSX settings. - **`.dockerignore` blocking .next**: If Docker COPY fails with "not found" for `.next/standalone/`, check that `.next/` is not in `.dockerignore`. Either remove it or use multi-stage build. - **Docker build fails after stack rollback**: Old service ID is cached. `docker stack rm` then deploy fresh. ### Placeholder Strategy When client data is missing, use WORKING placeholders (consistent dummy data) rather than `[PENDIENTE]` tags. Makes the site testable and reveals exactly what to replace. Every placeholder becomes a search-and-replace target. ## Reference: Live Audit Session Pattern The `references/live-ecommerce-session-example.md` file captures a real session transcript (Fun4Me) showing the exact terminal commands, batch flows, and diagnosis chain for a live ecommerce audit. Use it as a quick-reference template when running a new audit. ## Pitfalls 1. **Don't rewrite files** — use patch. Full rewrites break imports. 2. **Build before commit** — always `next build`, not just `tsc --noEmit`. Catches SSR/import issues. 3. **Supabase RLS** — admin writes via client-side need permissive RLS. Server API routes bypass with service role. 4. **Cart image_url** — fixing cart to include images requires updating BOTH the store type AND all addItem call sites. 5. **Telegram/WhatsApp group context** — keep responses concise, no markdown, focus on what's done + what's needed. 6. **Domain name discipline** — sitemap.ts, layout.ts metadataBase, OG URLs, site.json domain field must ALL agree. A single wrong URL causes Google indexing issues. {"code": "from hermes_tools import terminal

# Check what's serving CSS — Traefik or Next.js?

FROM node:20-alpine AS deps
WORKDIR /app
RUN apk add --no-cache libc6-compat
COPY package.json package-lock.json* ./
RUN npm ci --legacy-peer-deps

FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY . .
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
RUN npm run build

FROM node:20-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
ENV PORT=3000
ENV HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0"
RUN apk add --no-cache wget
RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs && \
    adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs
USER nextjs

COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/public ./public
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./content
CMD node server.js

Found the root cause. The `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` header is applied to `/(.*)` which includes static CSS/JS files. That header + Cloudflare is causing the browser to reject CSS served as `text/plain`.

The real issue: Next.js standalone output with `output: "standalone"` copies static files to `.next/static/`. The Next.js server should serve them with correct MIME types, but the `nosniff` header + Traefik is making the browser check. Let me check what's actually happening:

The CSS file `242423fcc7d32010.css` returns 404. It doesn't exist in the build. That's the real problem — a stale CSS hash referenced but the actual file has a different hash. Let me check what CSS files actually exist:

# Check what CSS files exist in the running container
result = terminal("docker ps -q -f name=elviajero 2>/dev/null", timeout=5)
containers = result.get('output', '').strip().split('\n')

for cid in containers:
    if cid:
        result = terminal(f"docker exec {cid} ls -la .next/static/css/ 2>&1", timeout=10)
        print(f"=== Container {cid[:12]} ===")
        print(result.get('output', ''))
        
        # Check what's in the CSS files
        result = terminal(f"docker exec {cid} head -3 .next/static/css/*.css 2>&1", timeout=10)
        print("=== CSS content preview ===")
        print(result.get('output', ''))

# Also check the build output css
result = terminal("ls -la /root/elviajero-comercio/.next/static/css/ 2>/dev/null", timeout=5)
print("=== LOCAL CSS ===")

print("=== BUILD MANIFEST ===")
print(result.get('output', ''))

function.name: execute_code
function.arguments:
{"code": "from hermes_tools import terminal

# Generate the PNG icons with Pillow
code = """
from PIL import Image, ImageDraw, ImageFont

# Create icon-192.png (192x192)
size = 192
img = Image.new('RGBA', (size, size), (27, 94, 32, 255))  # #1B5E20
draw = ImageDraw.Draw(img)

# Draw rounded rectangle background with corner radius
r = 36  # corner radius
draw.rounded_rectangle([(0,0), (size-1, size-1)], radius=r, fill=(27, 94, 32, 255))

# Simple text "EV" centered
# Try to find a font
try:
    font = ImageFont.truetype("/usr/share/fonts/truetype/dejavu/DejaVuSans-Bold.ttf", 80)
except:
    font = ImageFont.load_default()

# Center "EV" text
text = "EV"
bbox = draw.textbbox((0,0), text, font=font)
tw, th = bbox[2]-bbox[0], bbox[3]-bbox[1]
x = (size - tw) // 2 - bbox[0]
y = (size - th) // 2 - bbox[1]
draw.text((x, y), text, fill=(255,255,255,255), font=font)

img.save('/root/elviajero-comercio/public/images/icon-192.png', 'PNG')
print(f"Created icon-192.png: {size}x{size}")

# Create icon-512.png (512x512)
size2 = 512
img2 = Image.new('RGBA', (size2, size2), (27, 94, 32, 255))
draw2 = ImageDraw.Draw(img2)
draw2.rounded_rectangle([(0,0), (size2-1, size2-1)], radius=96, fill=(27, 94, 32, 255))

try:
    font2 = ImageFont.truetype("/usr/share/fonts/truetype/dejavu/DejaVuSans-Bold.ttf", 200)
except:
    font2 = ImageFont.load_default()

bbox2 = draw2.textbbox((0,0), text, font=font2)
tw2, th2 = bbox2[2]-bbox2[0], bbox2[3]-bbox2[1]
x2 = (size2 - tw2) // 2 - bbox2[0]
y2 = (size2 - th2) // 2 - bbox2[1]
draw2.text((x2, y2), text, fill=(255,255,255,255), font=font2)

img2.save('/root/elviajero-comercio/public/images/icon-512.png', 'PNG')
print(f"Created icon-512.png: {size2}x{size2}")
"""

import subprocess
result = subprocess.run(['python3', '-c', code], capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=15)
print(result.stdout)
if result.stderr:
    print("STDERR:", result.stderr)
"}

Review the conversation above and update the skill library. Be ACTIVE — most sessions produce at least one skill update, even if small. A pass that does nothing is a missed learning opportunity, not a neutral outcome.

Target shape of the library: CLASS-LEVEL skills, each with a rich SKILL.md and a `references/` directory for session-specific detail. Not a long flat list of narrow one-session-one-skill entries. This shapes HOW you update, not WHETHER you update.

Signals to look for (any one of these warrants action):
  • User corrected your style, tone, format, legibility, or verbosity. Frustration signals like 'stop doing X', 'this is too verbose', 'don't format like this', 'why are you explaining', 'just give me the answer', 'you always do Y and I hate it', or an explicit 'remember this' are FIRST-CLASS skill signals, not just memory signals. Update the relevant skill(s) to embed the preference so the next session starts already knowing.
  • User corrected your workflow, approach, or sequence of steps. Encode the correction as a pitfall or explicit step in the skill that governs that class of task.
  • Non-trivial technique, fix, workaround, debugging path, or tool-usage pattern emerged that a future session would benefit from. Capture it.
  • A skill that got loaded or consulted this session turned out to be wrong, missing a step, or outdated. Patch it NOW.

Preference order — prefer the earliest action that fits, but do pick one when a signal above fired:
  1. UPDATE A CURRENTLY-LOADED SKILL. Look back through the conversation for skills the user loaded via /skill-name or you read via skill_view. If any of them covers the territory of the new learning, PATCH that one first. It is the skill that was in play, so it's the right one to extend.
  2. UPDATE AN EXISTING UMBRELLA (via skills_list + skill_view). If no loaded skill fits but an existing class-level skill does, patch it. Add a subsection, a pitfall, or broaden a trigger.
  3. ADD A SUPPORT FILE under an existing umbrella. Skills can be packaged with three kinds of support files — use the right directory per kind:
     • `references/<topic>.md` — session-specific detail (error transcripts, reproduction recipes, provider quirks) AND condensed knowledge banks: quoted research, API docs, external authoritative excerpts, or domain notes you found while working on the problem. Write it concise and for the value of the task, not as a full mirror of upstream docs.
     • `templates/<name>.<ext>` — starter files meant to be copied and modified (boilerplate configs, scaffolding, a known-good example the agent can `reproduce with modifications`).
     • `scripts/<name>.<ext>` — statically re-runnable actions the skill can invoke directly (verification scripts, fixture generators, deterministic probes, anything the agent should run rather than hand-type each time).
     Add support files via skill_manage action=write_file with file_path starting 'references/', 'templates/', or 'scripts/'. The umbrella's SKILL.md should gain a one-line pointer to any new support file so future agents know it exists.
  4. CREATE A NEW CLASS-LEVEL UMBRELLA SKILL when no existing skill covers the class. The name MUST be at the class level. The name MUST NOT be a specific PR number, error string, feature codename, library-alone name, or 'fix-X / debug-Y / audit-Z-today' session artifact. If the proposed name only makes sense for today's task, it's wrong — fall back to (1), (2), or (3).

User-preference embedding (important): when the user expressed a style/format/workflow preference, the update belongs in the SKILL.md body, not just in memory. Memory captures 'who the user is and what the current situation and state of your operations are'; skills capture 'how to do this class of task for this user'. When they complain about how you handled a task, the skill that governs that task needs to carry the lesson.

If you notice two existing skills that overlap, note it in your reply — the background curator handles consolidation at scale.

'Nothing to save.' is a real option but should NOT be the default. If the session ran smoothly with no corrections and produced no new technique, just say 'Nothing to save.' and stop. Otherwise, act.
